questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is an isotope?
options: Atoms with different numbers of protons but the same number of neutrons.
Atoms with the same number of neutrons and electrons only.
Atoms that have lost all of their electrons.
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
questions.correctAnswer: Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons.
questions.explanation: Isotopes are atoms of the same element because they have the same number of protons, but they have different numbers of neutrons.
questions.senNote: Isotopes are atoms of the same element because they have the same number of protons, but they have different numbers of neutrons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement about nuclear equations is correct?
options: Only the number of neutrons has to balance.
Balancing nuclear equations is not needed for beta decay.
The total number of electrons must always stay unchanged in the nucleus.
The total mass number and total atomic number must balance on both sides of the equation.
questions.correctAnswer: The total mass number and total atomic number must balance on both sides of the equation.
questions.explanation: Nuclear equations are balanced by conserving both mass number and atomic number.
questions.senNote: Nuclear equations are balanced by conserving both mass number and atomic number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall the penetrating power of radiation
questions.question: Which type of radiation is stopped by paper?
options: Beta
Gamma
X-rays
Alpha
questions.explanation: Alpha radiation is the least penetrating and is stopped by paper or skin.
questions.senNote: Alpha radiation is the least penetrating and is stopped by paper or skin.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain one use of radioactive isotopes
questions.question: Why are isotopes of carbon useful in archaeology?
options: They always emit visible light when ancient objects are found.
Their protons change into electrons over time in a predictable way.
They are the only atoms that can be found in fossils.
Their half-lives allow the age of once-living materials to be estimated.
questions.correctAnswer: Their half-lives allow the age of once-living materials to be estimated.
questions.explanation: Radioactive dating uses the known half-life of isotopes such as carbon-14 to estimate age.
questions.senNote: Radioactive dating uses the known half-life of isotopes such as carbon-14 to estimate age.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain a medical use of radiation
questions.question: Why is gamma radiation useful for sterilising medical equipment?
options: It has a large mass and can be stored safely in plastic.
It can be seen with the human eye during treatment.
It always makes the equipment radioactive afterwards.
It kills microorganisms and can penetrate packaging.
questions.correctAnswer: It kills microorganisms and can penetrate packaging.
questions.explanation: Gamma radiation is penetrating and can destroy microorganisms without opening the packaging.
questions.senNote: Gamma radiation is penetrating and can destroy microorganisms without opening the packaging.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why the National Grid uses high potential difference
questions.question: Why is the National Grid useful for transferring electricity?
options: A lower potential difference increases the current and saves energy.
Transformers destroy energy so less heat is wasted.
The current is made larger so cables can be thinner.
A high potential difference reduces the current and therefore reduces energy losses.
questions.correctAnswer: A high potential difference reduces the current and therefore reduces energy losses.
questions.explanation: For the same power, a higher potential difference means a lower current, so less energy is wasted as heat.
questions.senNote: For the same power, a higher potential difference means a lower current, so less energy is wasted as heat.

questions.question: Which statement about current in a series circuit is correct?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A Sankey diagram shows a wide useful arrow and a narrow wasted arrow. What does this mean?
options: The device has zero energy input.
The device destroys most of the energy.
The device does no useful work.
The device is fairly efficient.
questions.explanation: A larger useful output compared with wasted output means a higher efficiency.
questions.senNote: A larger useful output compared with wasted output means a higher efficiency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a ball falls, which energy store decreases most clearly?
options: The nuclear energy store
The elastic energy store only
The magnetic energy store
The gravitational potential energy store
questions.explanation: As a ball falls, gravitational potential energy decreases and kinetic energy usually increases.
questions.senNote: As a ball falls, gravitational potential energy decreases and kinetic energy usually increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain how insulation reduces energy transfer
questions.question: Why does thicker loft insulation reduce energy transfer from a house?
options: It increases the temperature difference across the roof.
It makes convection currents stronger.
It creates more energy inside the roof.
It slows the rate of thermal energy transfer by conduction.
questions.correctAnswer: It slows the rate of thermal energy transfer by conduction.
questions.explanation: Insulation traps air and reduces conduction, which slows thermal energy transfer.
questions.senNote: Insulation traps air and reduces conduction, which slows thermal energy transfer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a rocket pushes gas downwards, which answer shows Newton's third law?
options: Only the larger object experiences a force.
Both forces act on the same object in the same direction.
The second force only appears if the object speeds up.
The rocket is pushed upwards by the gas.
questions.correctAnswer: The rocket is pushed upwards by the gas.
questions.explanation: Action and reaction forces are equal in size, opposite in direction, and act on different objects.
questions.senNote: Action and reaction forces are equal in size, opposite in direction, and act on different objects.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a swimmer pushes water backwards, which answer shows Newton's third law?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the role of slip rings in an AC generator
questions.question: What is the purpose of the slip rings in an AC generator?
options: They store charge so the output stays direct current.
They make the magnetic field disappear every half turn.
They increase the resistance of the coil to zero.
They allow the coil to keep rotating while the output alternates.
questions.correctAnswer: They allow the coil to keep rotating while the output alternates.
questions.explanation: Slip rings maintain contact while allowing the current to alternate as the coil turns.
questions.senNote: Slip rings maintain contact while allowing the current to alternate as the coil turns.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the field around a current-carrying wire
questions.question: What is the shape of the magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire?
options: Straight lines pointing away from the wire
A single line only above the wire
A field that is always zero outside the wire
Concentric circles around the wire
questions.explanation: The magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire forms concentric circles.
questions.senNote: The magnetic field around a straight current-carrying wire forms concentric circles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare magnetically soft and hard materials
questions.question: Which statement about a magnetically soft material is correct?
options: It is hard to magnetise but easy to demagnetise.
It stays magnetised strongly and is best for permanent magnets.
It cannot be used in an electromagnet.
It is easy to magnetise and easy to demagnetise.
questions.correctAnswer: It is easy to magnetise and easy to demagnetise.
questions.explanation: Magnetically soft iron is useful in electromagnets because it magnetises and demagnetises easily.
questions.senNote: Magnetically soft iron is useful in electromagnets because it magnetises and demagnetises easily.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Predict attraction and repulsion between poles
questions.question: What happens when the north pole of one magnet is brought near the north pole of another magnet?
options: They attract each other strongly.
One magnet loses all its magnetism at once.
They join together and make a neutral magnet.
They repel each other.
questions.explanation: Like poles repel and unlike poles attract.
questions.senNote: Like poles repel and unlike poles attract.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A transformer has 220 turns on the primary coil and 60 turns on the secondary coil. The primary voltage is 140 V. What is the secondary voltage?
questions.explanation: For an ideal transformer, primary voltage divided by secondary voltage equals primary turns divided by secondary turns.
questions.senNote: For an ideal transformer, primary voltage divided by secondary voltage equals primary turns divided by secondary turns.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is liquid water denser than steam?
options: The particles in steam are heavier than the particles in water.
The liquid particles stop moving completely.
Steam contains no energy so it spreads out.
The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.correctAnswer: The particles are much closer together in the liquid.
questions.explanation: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.
questions.senNote: Density depends on how much mass is packed into a given volume. Liquid particles are closer together than gas particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which statement best describes internal energy?
options: It is only the energy stored in chemical bonds.
It is the same as gravitational potential energy only.
It is zero whenever a substance is not moving through space.
It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.correctAnswer: It is the total kinetic energy and potential energy of the particles in a system.
questions.explanation: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.
questions.senNote: Internal energy includes both the kinetic and potential energy of the particles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall the life cycle of a Sun-like star
questions.question: What happens to a star like the Sun after the red giant stage?
options: It becomes a supernova and then a neutron star.
It turns straight into a black hole.
It becomes a main-sequence star again.
It forms a planetary nebula and then becomes a white dwarf.
questions.correctAnswer: It forms a planetary nebula and then becomes a white dwarf.
questions.explanation: A Sun-like star becomes a red giant, then a planetary nebula, and finally a white dwarf.
questions.senNote: A Sun-like star becomes a red giant, then a planetary nebula, and finally a white dwarf.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why are optical telescopes often placed on mountains or in space?
options: Gravity is weaker there so the telescopes can see further.
The telescopes need warmer temperatures to work properly.
There is more sound in the air to carry the signals.
There is less atmosphere to distort or absorb the light.
questions.correctAnswer: There is less atmosphere to distort or absorb the light.
questions.explanation: Less atmosphere means clearer observations because there is less absorption and distortion of light.
questions.senNote: Less atmosphere means clearer observations because there is less absorption and distortion of light.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why do astronauts appear weightless in orbit?
options: There is no gravity at all where satellites orbit.
Their masses become zero once they leave the atmosphere.
The spacecraft pushes them upwards harder than Earth pulls them down.
They are in free-fall while moving forwards around Earth.

questions.explanation: Astronauts and the spacecraft are both falling together around Earth, so they appear weightless.
questions.senNote: Astronauts and the spacecraft are both falling together around Earth, so they appear weightless.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What does red-shift suggest about many distant galaxies?
options: They are becoming cooler than all nearby stars only.
They are moving towards us faster than light.
They have stopped emitting electromagnetic waves.
They are moving away from us.
questions.correctAnswer: They are moving away from us.
questions.explanation: Red-shift shows that light has stretched, which happens when a source is moving away from us.
questions.senNote: Red-shift shows that light has stretched, which happens when a source is moving away from us.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Why is ultrasound useful for scanning inside the human body?
options: It is ionising so it passes through all tissue.
It travels only through a vacuum.
It always carries more energy than X-rays.
It can be reflected to make images and it is non-ionising.
questions.correctAnswer: It can be reflected to make images and it is non-ionising.
questions.explanation: Ultrasound reflects at boundaries between tissues and is safe because it is non-ionising.
questions.senNote: Ultrasound reflects at boundaries between tissues and is safe because it is non-ionising.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is the unit of electric charge?
options: C
A
V
W
questions.explanation: Charge is measured in coulombs, written as C. A common mistake is to choose A, which is the unit of current.
questions.question: What is the unit of electric charge?
questions.senNote: Charge is measured in coulombs, written as C. A common mistake is to choose A, which is the unit of current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate charge from current and time
questions.stem: A current of 4 A flows for 6 s. How much charge is transferred?
options: 2 C
questions.explanation: Use Q = I x t, so 4 x 6 = 24 C. A common mistake is to add the two numbers.
questions.question: A current of 4 A flows for 6 s. How much charge is transferred?
questions.senNote: Use Q = I x t, so 4 x 6 = 24 C. A common mistake is to add the two numbers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate time from charge and current
questions.stem: A tablet transfers 90 C of charge with a current of 3 A. How long does this take?
questions.explanation: Rearrange Q = I x t to t = Q / I. So 90 / 3 = 30 s.
questions.question: A tablet transfers 90 C of charge with a current of 3 A. How long does this take?
questions.senNote: Rearrange Q = I x t to t = Q / I. So 90 / 3 = 30 s.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare charge transferred in two circuits
questions.stem: Circuit A carries 6 A for 4 s. Circuit B carries 2 A for 15 s. Which circuit transfers more charge?
options: Circuit B transfers more charge.
Circuit A transfers more charge.
They transfer the same charge.
Charge cannot be compared without resistance.
questions.explanation: Circuit A transfers 24 C and Circuit B transfers 30 C. A common mistake is to look only at the bigger current.
questions.question: Circuit A carries 6 A for 4 s. Circuit B carries 2 A for 15 s. Which circuit transfers more charge?
questions.senNote: Circuit A transfers 24 C and Circuit B transfers 30 C. A common mistake is to look only at the bigger current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about charge and current
questions.stem: A student says, "A bigger current always means more charge has transferred." Which response is best?
options: That is not always correct, because the time also matters.
That is always correct, because current and charge are the same.
That is always correct, because time does not affect charge.
That is not correct, because charge depends only on voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not always correct, because the time also matters.
questions.explanation: Charge depends on both current and time. A common mistake is to use current alone to judge charge.
questions.question: A student says, "A bigger current always means more charge has transferred." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Charge depends on both current and time. A common mistake is to use current alone to judge charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What does electric current measure?
options: The rate of flow of charge
The total resistance in a circuit
The energy stored in a cell
The force between two magnets
questions.correctAnswer: The rate of flow of charge
questions.explanation: Current tells how much charge flows each second. A common mistake is to confuse current with stored energy.
questions.question: What does electric current measure?
questions.senNote: Current tells how much charge flows each second. A common mistake is to confuse current with stored energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which unit is used for electric current?
options: A
C
J
ohm
questions.explanation: Current is measured in amperes, written as A. A common mistake is to choose C, the unit of charge.
questions.question: Which unit is used for electric current?
questions.senNote: Current is measured in amperes, written as A. A common mistake is to choose C, the unit of charge.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate current from charge and time
questions.stem: A meter shows that 36 C of charge passes in 12 s. What is the current?
options: 3 A
questions.explanation: Use I = Q / t, so 36 / 12 = 3 A. A common mistake is to multiply charge by time.
questions.question: A meter shows that 36 C of charge passes in 12 s. What is the current?
questions.senNote: Use I = Q / t, so 36 / 12 = 3 A. A common mistake is to multiply charge by time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A student needs to measure the current through a resistor. What should they do?
options: Connect an ammeter in series with the resistor.
Connect a voltmeter in series with the resistor.
Connect an ammeter in parallel across the resistor.
Connect a ruler next to the resistor.
questions.correctAnswer: Connect an ammeter in series with the resistor.
questions.explanation: An ammeter measures current and is connected in series. A common mistake is to connect it in parallel.
questions.question: A student needs to measure the current through a resistor. What should they do?
questions.senNote: An ammeter measures current and is connected in series. A common mistake is to connect it in parallel.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Interpret current readings in a circuit
questions.stem: In a series circuit, an ammeter before a bulb reads 0.5 A. An ammeter after the bulb reads 0.5 A. What does this show?
options: Current is the same before and after the bulb.
The bulb has used up half the current.
Current only flows before the bulb.
The second ammeter is measuring voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: Current is the same before and after the bulb.
questions.explanation: Current is the same at all points in a series circuit. A common mistake is to think components use up current.
questions.question: In a series circuit, an ammeter before a bulb reads 0.5 A. An ammeter after the bulb reads 0.5 A. What does this show?
questions.senNote: Current is the same at all points in a series circuit. A common mistake is to think components use up current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What does potential difference tell you?
options: Energy transferred per unit charge
Charge flowing per second
Resistance per metre
Power transferred per ampere
questions.explanation: Potential difference is energy transferred per coulomb of charge. A common mistake is to confuse it with current.
questions.question: What does potential difference tell you?
questions.senNote: Potential difference is energy transferred per coulomb of charge. A common mistake is to confuse it with current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which unit is used for potential difference?
options: V
A
C
kg
questions.explanation: Potential difference is measured in volts, written as V. A common mistake is to choose A, the unit of current.
questions.question: Which unit is used for potential difference?
questions.senNote: Potential difference is measured in volts, written as V. A common mistake is to choose A, the unit of current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose how to measure potential difference
questions.stem: How should a voltmeter be connected to measure the potential difference across a lamp?
options: In parallel across the lamp
In series with the lamp
In parallel across the switch only
In series with the cell only
questions.explanation: A voltmeter measures across a component, so it is connected in parallel. A common mistake is to put it in series.
questions.question: How should a voltmeter be connected to measure the potential difference across a lamp?
questions.senNote: A voltmeter measures across a component, so it is connected in parallel. A common mistake is to put it in series.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A 12 V battery is connected to two identical lamps in series. What is the potential difference across each lamp?
options: About 6 V
0 V
questions.explanation: In series, the supply potential difference is shared between components. A common mistake is to give each lamp the full battery voltage.
questions.question: A 12 V battery is connected to two identical lamps in series. What is the potential difference across each lamp?
questions.senNote: In series, the supply potential difference is shared between components. A common mistake is to give each lamp the full battery voltage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about potential difference
questions.stem: A student says, "Voltage is used up as it travels around the wires." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because potential difference is across components, not used up in the wires.
That is correct, because voltage is a type of fuel.
That is correct, because wires always use all the voltage.
That is not correct, because voltage is the same as current.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct, because potential difference is across components, not used up in the wires.
questions.explanation: Potential difference is measured across components. A common mistake is to think voltage is a substance that gets used up.
questions.question: A student says, "Voltage is used up as it travels around the wires." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Potential difference is measured across components. A common mistake is to think voltage is a substance that gets used up.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is the unit of resistance?
options: ohm
A
V
C
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in ohms. A common mistake is to choose V, the unit of potential difference.
questions.question: What is the unit of resistance?
questions.senNote: Resistance is measured in ohms. A common mistake is to choose V, the unit of potential difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate current using V = I x R
questions.stem: A resistor has 10 V across it and a resistance of 5 ohm. What current flows?
options: 2 A
questions.explanation: Use I = V / R, so 10 / 5 = 2 A. A common mistake is to multiply voltage by resistance.
questions.question: A resistor has 10 V across it and a resistance of 5 ohm. What current flows?
questions.senNote: Use I = V / R, so 10 / 5 = 2 A. A common mistake is to multiply voltage by resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate resistance from voltage and current
questions.stem: A voltmeter reads 8 V and an ammeter reads 0.4 A for a component. What is the resistance?
options: 20 ohm
3.2 ohm
8.4 ohm
0.05 ohm
questions.explanation: Use R = V / I, so 8 / 0.4 = 20 ohm. A common mistake is to multiply the readings.
questions.question: A voltmeter reads 8 V and an ammeter reads 0.4 A for a component. What is the resistance?
questions.senNote: Use R = V / I, so 8 / 0.4 = 20 ohm. A common mistake is to multiply the readings.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Two resistors have the same potential difference across them. Resistor X has a larger resistance than resistor Y. Which statement is correct?
options: Resistor X has the smaller current.
Resistor X has the larger current.
Both resistors must have zero current.
Both resistors must have the same current.
questions.correctAnswer: Resistor X has the smaller current.
questions.explanation: For the same potential difference, a larger resistance gives a smaller current. A common mistake is to think larger resistance gives larger current.
questions.question: Two resistors have the same potential difference across them. Resistor X has a larger resistance than resistor Y. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: For the same potential difference, a larger resistance gives a smaller current. A common mistake is to think larger resistance gives larger current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Interpret an ohmic conductor from readings
questions.stem: A resistor has 3 V and 0.6 A in one test. In another test it has 6 V and 1.2 A. Which statement is correct?
options: The resistance is the same in both tests.
The resistance doubles in the second test.
The resistance halves in the second test.
Resistance cannot be found from these readings.
questions.correctAnswer: The resistance is the same in both tests.
questions.explanation: The resistance is 3 / 0.6 = 5 ohm and 6 / 1.2 = 5 ohm. A common mistake is to look only at the larger voltage.
questions.question: A resistor has 3 V and 0.6 A in one test. In another test it has 6 V and 1.2 A. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: The resistance is 3 / 0.6 = 5 ohm and 6 / 1.2 = 5 ohm. A common mistake is to look only at the larger voltage.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is true about current in a series circuit?
options: The current is the same at every point.
The current is used up by each component.
The current is largest after the last component.
The current splits between components.
questions.correctAnswer: The current is the same at every point.
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is one path for charge. A common mistake is to think current is used up.
questions.question: What is true about current in a series circuit?
questions.senNote: In a series circuit, there is one path for charge. A common mistake is to think current is used up.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: An ammeter near the cell in a series circuit reads 0.9 A. What should an ammeter after the second lamp read?
options: 0 A
questions.explanation: Current is the same everywhere in a series circuit. A common mistake is to halve the current after two lamps.
questions.question: An ammeter near the cell in a series circuit reads 0.9 A. What should an ammeter after the second lamp read?
questions.senNote: Current is the same everywhere in a series circuit. A common mistake is to halve the current after two lamps.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose a method to test current in series
questions.stem: A student wants to test whether current is the same around a series circuit. What is the best method?
options: Move an ammeter to different points in series and compare readings.
Use a voltmeter across each wire only.
Put an ammeter in parallel with each lamp.
Measure only the battery voltage.
questions.correctAnswer: Move an ammeter to different points in series and compare readings.
questions.explanation: An ammeter must be in series to measure current. A common mistake is to connect the ammeter in parallel.
questions.question: A student wants to test whether current is the same around a series circuit. What is the best method?
questions.senNote: An ammeter must be in series to measure current. A common mistake is to connect the ammeter in parallel.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare current readings in series circuits
questions.stem: Series circuit X has a current of 0.2 A. Series circuit Y has a current of 0.7 A. Which statement is correct?
options: Every point in circuit Y has a current of 0.7 A.
Circuit Y has 0.7 A only near the cell.
Both circuits must have the same current because both are series.
Circuit X has more current after each component.
questions.correctAnswer: Every point in circuit Y has a current of 0.7 A.
questions.explanation: Current is the same within each series circuit. A common mistake is to think all series circuits have the same current.
questions.question: Series circuit X has a current of 0.2 A. Series circuit Y has a current of 0.7 A. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: Current is the same within each series circuit. A common mistake is to think all series circuits have the same current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about current in series
questions.stem: A student says, "A lamp farther from the battery gets less current." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because current is the same everywhere in a series circuit.
That is correct, because current gets weaker with distance.
That is correct, because the first lamp keeps most of the current.
That is not correct, because only voltage exists in a series circuit.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct, because current is the same everywhere in a series circuit.
questions.explanation: Distance from the cell does not change the series current. A common mistake is to think current is used up along the circuit.
questions.question: A student says, "A lamp farther from the battery gets less current." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Distance from the cell does not change the series current. A common mistake is to think current is used up along the circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: How do you find the total resistance of resistors in series?
options: Add the resistances together.
Subtract the smallest resistance.
Divide each resistance by the voltage.
Use only the largest resistance.
questions.explanation: Resistances in series add together. A common mistake is to use only one resistor value.
questions.question: How do you find the total resistance of resistors in series?
questions.senNote: Resistances in series add together. A common mistake is to use only one resistor value.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A 6 ohm resistor and an 11 ohm resistor are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
options: 17 ohm
66 ohm
5 ohm
1.8 ohm
questions.explanation: For series resistors, add the values: 6 + 11 = 17 ohm. A common mistake is to multiply them.
questions.question: A 6 ohm resistor and an 11 ohm resistor are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
questions.senNote: For series resistors, add the values: 6 + 11 = 17 ohm. A common mistake is to multiply them.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Three resistors of 4 ohm, 9 ohm and 13 ohm are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
options: 26 ohm
468 ohm
13 ohm
8.7 ohm
questions.explanation: Add all three resistances: 4 + 9 + 13 = 26 ohm. A common mistake is to average the values.
questions.question: Three resistors of 4 ohm, 9 ohm and 13 ohm are connected in series. What is the total resistance?
questions.senNote: Add all three resistances: 4 + 9 + 13 = 26 ohm. A common mistake is to average the values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain effect of adding resistance in series
questions.stem: A resistor is added in series with a lamp. The supply voltage stays the same. What happens to the current?
options: The current decreases.
The current increases.
The current becomes zero in all cases.
The current stays the same because the voltage stays the same.
questions.explanation: Adding resistance in series increases total resistance. A common mistake is to think the same voltage always gives the same current.
questions.question: A resistor is added in series with a lamp. The supply voltage stays the same. What happens to the current?
questions.senNote: Adding resistance in series increases total resistance. A common mistake is to think the same voltage always gives the same current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A series circuit has a total resistance of 32 ohm. Two resistors are 7 ohm and 18 ohm. What is the resistance of the third resistor?
options: 7 ohm
57 ohm
25 ohm
10.7 ohm
questions.explanation: The known resistances add to 25 ohm, so the missing value is 32 - 25 = 7 ohm. A common mistake is to add all given numbers.
questions.question: A series circuit has a total resistance of 32 ohm. Two resistors are 7 ohm and 18 ohm. What is the resistance of the third resistor?
questions.senNote: The known resistances add to 25 ohm, so the missing value is 32 - 25 = 7 ohm. A common mistake is to add all given numbers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which statement describes a parallel circuit?
options: It has more than one path for current.
It has only one path for current.
It cannot contain lamps.
It has no potential difference.
questions.correctAnswer: It has more than one path for current.
questions.explanation: A parallel circuit has separate branches. A common mistake is to describe it as a series circuit.
questions.question: Which statement describes a parallel circuit?
questions.senNote: A parallel circuit has separate branches. A common mistake is to describe it as a series circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Two lamps are connected in parallel. One branch is switched off. What can happen to the other lamp?
options: It can stay on.
It must switch off.
It must become a resistor.
It must get no voltage.
questions.explanation: Parallel branches can work independently. A common mistake is to think one break stops the whole circuit.
questions.question: Two lamps are connected in parallel. One branch is switched off. What can happen to the other lamp?
questions.senNote: Parallel branches can work independently. A common mistake is to think one break stops the whole circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A parallel circuit has branch currents of 0.2 A, 0.5 A and 0.8 A. What is the total current?
questions.explanation: Add the branch currents: 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.8 = 1.5 A. A common mistake is to use only the largest branch current.
questions.question: A parallel circuit has branch currents of 0.2 A, 0.5 A and 0.8 A. What is the total current?
questions.senNote: Add the branch currents: 0.2 + 0.5 + 0.8 = 1.5 A. A common mistake is to use only the largest branch current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recognise potential difference in parallel branches
questions.stem: A 6 V cell is connected to three branches in parallel. What is the potential difference across each branch?
options: 6 V
2 V
0 V
questions.explanation: Each parallel branch has the full supply potential difference. A common mistake is to divide the voltage between branches.
questions.question: A 6 V cell is connected to three branches in parallel. What is the potential difference across each branch?
questions.senNote: Each parallel branch has the full supply potential difference. A common mistake is to divide the voltage between branches.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about parallel current
questions.stem: A student says, "Current must split equally in all parallel branches." Which response is best?
options: That is not always correct, because branch resistance can be different.
That is always correct, because parallel branches are identical.
That is always correct, because current cannot change.
That is not correct, because parallel circuits have no current.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not always correct, because branch resistance can be different.
questions.explanation: Current can split unevenly if branches have different resistances. A common mistake is to assume every branch is the same.
questions.question: A student says, "Current must split equally in all parallel branches." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Current can split unevenly if branches have different resistances. A common mistake is to assume every branch is the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What does power mean?
options: The rate of energy transfer
The total charge transferred
The resistance of a wire
The time taken for charge to stop
questions.explanation: Power tells how quickly energy is transferred. A common mistake is to confuse power with total energy.
questions.question: What does power mean?
questions.senNote: Power tells how quickly energy is transferred. A common mistake is to confuse power with total energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which unit is used for power?
options: W
J
C
s
questions.explanation: Power is measured in watts, written as W. A common mistake is to choose J, the unit of energy.
questions.question: Which unit is used for power?
questions.senNote: Power is measured in watts, written as W. A common mistake is to choose J, the unit of energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate power from energy and time
questions.stem: A radio transfers 600 J of energy in 20 s. What is its power?
questions.explanation: Use P = E / t, so 600 / 20 = 30 W. A common mistake is to multiply energy by time.
questions.question: A radio transfers 600 J of energy in 20 s. What is its power?
questions.senNote: Use P = E / t, so 600 / 20 = 30 W. A common mistake is to multiply energy by time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Compare power using energy and time
questions.stem: Two drills transfer the same amount of energy. Drill A takes 15 s and Drill B takes 45 s. Which drill has the greater power?
options: Drill A
Drill B
They have the same power.
Power cannot be compared without resistance.
questions.explanation: The same energy transferred in less time means greater power. A common mistake is to think longer time means greater power.
questions.question: Two drills transfer the same amount of energy. Drill A takes 15 s and Drill B takes 45 s. Which drill has the greater power?
questions.senNote: The same energy transferred in less time means greater power. A common mistake is to think longer time means greater power.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A student says, "An appliance with a higher power always transfers more energy." Which response is best?
options: That is not always correct, because the time it is used for also matters.
That is always correct, because power and energy are the same.
That is always correct, because time does not affect energy.
That is not correct, because power is measured in coulombs.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not always correct, because the time it is used for also matters.
questions.explanation: Energy transferred depends on power and time. A common mistake is to compare power without considering time.
questions.question: A student says, "An appliance with a higher power always transfers more energy." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Energy transferred depends on power and time. A common mistake is to compare power without considering time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify an energy store in a battery device
questions.stem: A battery-powered torch is switched on. What is the main starting energy store?
options: Chemical store in the battery
Thermal store in the bulb
Gravitational store in the torch
Nuclear store in the wires
questions.explanation: A battery stores energy chemically. A common mistake is to think the bulb stores the starting energy.
questions.question: A battery-powered torch is switched on. What is the main starting energy store?
questions.senNote: A battery stores energy chemically. A common mistake is to think the bulb stores the starting energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which statement about energy in an electrical appliance is correct?
options: Energy is transferred from one store to another.
Energy is created by the appliance.
Energy disappears when the appliance heats up.
Energy can only transfer as sound.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is transferred from one store to another.
questions.explanation: Energy is conserved and transferred between stores. A common mistake is to think energy is created or destroyed.
questions.question: Which statement about energy in an electrical appliance is correct?
questions.senNote: Energy is conserved and transferred between stores. A common mistake is to think energy is created or destroyed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate energy transferred from power and time
questions.stem: A 25 W lamp is on for 80 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.explanation: Use E = P x t, so 25 x 80 = 2000 J. A common mistake is to divide by time.
questions.question: A 25 W lamp is on for 80 s. How much energy is transferred?
questions.senNote: Use E = P x t, so 25 x 80 = 2000 J. A common mistake is to divide by time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify useful and wasted energy transfers
questions.stem: An electric blender is used to mix food. Which statement is correct?
options: Useful energy is transferred to the moving blades, with some energy wasted as sound and heating.
All energy is wasted because the blender makes sound.
Energy is destroyed by the motor.
The useful energy output is mainly gravitational.
questions.correctAnswer: Useful energy is transferred to the moving blades, with some energy wasted as sound and heating.
questions.explanation: The useful transfer is to kinetic energy of the blades. A common mistake is to think sound means all energy is wasted.
questions.question: An electric blender is used to mix food. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: The useful transfer is to kinetic energy of the blades. A common mistake is to think sound means all energy is wasted.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Two heaters both have a power of 150 W. Heater X runs for 30 s and Heater Y runs for 70 s. Which statement is correct?
options: Heater Y transfers more energy.
Heater X transfers more energy.
They transfer the same energy.
Energy cannot be compared without current.
questions.explanation: With the same power, the heater used for longer transfers more energy. A common mistake is to think equal power means equal energy in all cases.
questions.question: Two heaters both have a power of 150 W. Heater X runs for 30 s and Heater Y runs for 70 s. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: With the same power, the heater used for longer transfers more energy. A common mistake is to think equal power means equal energy in all cases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Recall the purpose of the National Grid
questions.stem: What is the main purpose of the National Grid?
options: To transfer electricity from power stations to users
To store electricity inside pylons
To make all homes use the same current
To turn all electricity into sound
questions.correctAnswer: To transfer electricity from power stations to users
questions.explanation: The National Grid transfers electrical power around the country. A common mistake is to think it stores electricity.
questions.question: What is the main purpose of the National Grid?
questions.senNote: The National Grid transfers electrical power around the country. A common mistake is to think it stores electricity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What do transformers change in the National Grid?
options: Potential difference
Mass
Density
Time
questions.explanation: Transformers increase or decrease potential difference. A common mistake is to think they change mass or time.
questions.question: What do transformers change in the National Grid?
questions.senNote: Transformers increase or decrease potential difference. A common mistake is to think they change mass or time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which sequence is used in the National Grid?
options: Step-up transformer for transmission, then step-down transformer for homes
Step-down transformer for transmission, then step-up transformer for homes
Only step-up transformers everywhere
No transformers are used
questions.correctAnswer: Step-up transformer for transmission, then step-down transformer for homes
questions.explanation: Voltage is stepped up for transmission and stepped down before use. A common mistake is to swap the transformer roles.
questions.question: Which sequence is used in the National Grid?
questions.senNote: Voltage is stepped up for transmission and stepped down before use. A common mistake is to swap the transformer roles.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain why high voltage is used
questions.stem: Why does the National Grid transmit electricity at a high potential difference?
options: To reduce current and reduce heating losses in cables
To increase current and heat the cables more
To remove all resistance from the cables
To stop energy being transferred
questions.correctAnswer: To reduce current and reduce heating losses in cables
questions.explanation: For the same power, a higher potential difference means a lower current. A common mistake is to think high voltage removes resistance.
questions.question: Why does the National Grid transmit electricity at a high potential difference?
questions.senNote: For the same power, a higher potential difference means a lower current. A common mistake is to think high voltage removes resistance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about energy losses
questions.stem: A student says, "Using high voltage means no energy is wasted in the cables." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because high voltage reduces losses but does not remove them completely.
That is correct, because high voltage makes resistance zero.
That is correct, because current stops flowing.
That is not correct, because transformers only change charge.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct, because high voltage reduces losses but does not remove them completely.
questions.explanation: Cables still have resistance, so some energy can be wasted by heating. A common mistake is to think high voltage removes all losses.
questions.question: A student says, "Using high voltage means no energy is wasted in the cables." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Cables still have resistance, so some energy can be wasted by heating. A common mistake is to think high voltage removes all losses.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which statement describes a scalar quantity?
options: It has size but no direction.
It has direction but no size.
It always has a force acting on it.
It can only be measured in newtons.
questions.correctAnswer: It has size but no direction.
questions.explanation: A scalar has magnitude only. A common mistake is to think all measurements need a direction.
questions.question: Which statement describes a scalar quantity?
questions.senNote: A scalar has magnitude only. A common mistake is to think all measurements need a direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which quantity is a vector?
options: Force
Mass
Time
Distance
questions.explanation: Force has size and direction, so it is a vector. A common mistake is to choose mass, which is a scalar.
questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.senNote: Force has size and direction, so it is a vector. A common mistake is to choose mass, which is a scalar.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A runner goes 100 m east, then 100 m west back to the start. Which statement is correct?
options: The distance is 200 m and the displacement is 0 m.
The distance is 0 m and the displacement is 200 m east.
The distance and displacement are both 200 m.
The distance and displacement are both 0 m.
questions.correctAnswer: The distance is 200 m and the displacement is 0 m.
questions.explanation: Distance is the total path travelled. Displacement is the straight-line change in position, so it is zero here.
questions.question: A runner goes 100 m east, then 100 m west back to the start. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: Distance is the total path travelled. Displacement is the straight-line change in position, so it is zero here.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which statement about speed and velocity is correct?
options: Velocity includes direction, but speed does not.
Speed includes direction, but velocity does not.
Both speed and velocity are scalars.
Velocity is measured in newtons.
questions.correctAnswer: Velocity includes direction, but speed does not.
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector because it has direction. A common mistake is to treat speed and velocity as exactly the same.
questions.question: Which statement about speed and velocity is correct?
questions.senNote: Velocity is a vector because it has direction. A common mistake is to treat speed and velocity as exactly the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A student says, "If two forces have the same size, they must be the same force." Which response is best?
options: That is not always correct, because force also has direction.
That is always correct, because only size matters for forces.
That is always correct, because force is a scalar.
That is not correct, because forces do not have size.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not always correct, because force also has direction.
questions.explanation: Force is a vector, so direction matters. A common mistake is to compare only the size of a force.
questions.question: A student says, "If two forces have the same size, they must be the same force." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Force is a vector, so direction matters. A common mistake is to compare only the size of a force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which force is a contact force?
options: Friction
Gravity
Magnetic force
Electrostatic force
questions.explanation: Friction needs surfaces to touch. A common mistake is to think gravity needs contact.
questions.question: Which force is a contact force?
questions.senNote: Friction needs surfaces to touch. A common mistake is to think gravity needs contact.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is a non-contact force?
options: A force that acts without objects touching.
A force that only acts between touching surfaces.
A force that always slows objects down.
A force that has no direction.
questions.correctAnswer: A force that acts without objects touching.
questions.explanation: Non-contact forces can act at a distance. A common mistake is to think all forces need contact.
questions.question: What is a non-contact force?
questions.senNote: Non-contact forces can act at a distance. A common mistake is to think all forces need contact.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Identify forces acting on an object
questions.stem: A book rests on a table. Which pair of forces act on the book?
options: Weight and normal contact force
Friction and magnetic force only
Air resistance and thrust only
Upthrust and electrostatic force only
questions.explanation: The book has weight downward and a normal contact force upward. A common mistake is to ignore the contact force from the table.
questions.question: A book rests on a table. Which pair of forces act on the book?
questions.senNote: The book has weight downward and a normal contact force upward. A common mistake is to ignore the contact force from the table.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A magnet pulls a paper clip across a desk. Which statement is correct?
options: Magnetic force is non-contact, but friction is contact.
Both magnetic force and friction are non-contact.
Magnetic force is contact, but friction is non-contact.
Neither magnetic force nor friction is a force.
questions.correctAnswer: Magnetic force is non-contact, but friction is contact.
questions.explanation: Magnetic force can act without touching. Friction needs contact between surfaces.
questions.question: A magnet pulls a paper clip across a desk. Which statement is correct?
questions.senNote: Magnetic force can act without touching. Friction needs contact between surfaces.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A student says, "Gravity only acts when objects touch the Earth." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because gravity is a non-contact force.
That is correct, because gravity needs contact.
That is correct, because gravity is friction.
That is not correct, because gravity has no direction.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct, because gravity is a non-contact force.
questions.explanation: Gravity acts at a distance between masses. A common mistake is to think weight needs touching.
questions.question: A student says, "Gravity only acts when objects touch the Earth." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Gravity acts at a distance between masses. A common mistake is to think weight needs touching.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate resultant force in the same direction
questions.stem: Two people push a box to the right with forces of 20 N and 15 N. What is the resultant force?
options: 35 N to the right
5 N to the right
35 N to the left
300 N to the right
questions.explanation: Forces in the same direction are added. A common mistake is to subtract them.
questions.question: Two people push a box to the right with forces of 20 N and 15 N. What is the resultant force?
questions.senNote: Forces in the same direction are added. A common mistake is to subtract them.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is the resultant force when forces are balanced?
options: 0 N
1 N
The largest force
The sum of all forces
questions.explanation: Balanced forces have no overall resultant force. A common mistake is to choose the largest force.
questions.question: What is the resultant force when forces are balanced?
questions.senNote: Balanced forces have no overall resultant force. A common mistake is to choose the largest force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate resultant force in opposite directions
questions.stem: A trolley is pulled forwards with 50 N. Friction acts backwards with 18 N. What is the resultant force?
options: 32 N forwards
68 N forwards
32 N backwards
900 N forwards
questions.explanation: Opposite forces are subtracted: 50 - 18 = 32 N. The direction is forwards because 50 N is larger.
questions.question: A trolley is pulled forwards with 50 N. Friction acts backwards with 18 N. What is the resultant force?
questions.senNote: Opposite forces are subtracted: 50 - 18 = 32 N. The direction is forwards because 50 N is larger.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the effect of a resultant force
questions.stem: A cyclist has a resultant force forwards. What will happen to the cyclist?
options: The cyclist will accelerate forwards.
The cyclist must stop immediately.
The cyclist will move at constant speed.
The cyclist will have zero velocity.
questions.explanation: A non-zero resultant force causes acceleration. A common mistake is to think any force means constant speed.
questions.question: A cyclist has a resultant force forwards. What will happen to the cyclist?
questions.senNote: A non-zero resultant force causes acceleration. A common mistake is to think any force means constant speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about resultant force
questions.stem: A student says, "If a car is moving, there must be a resultant force forwards." Which response is best?
options: That is not always correct, because a car can move at constant speed with zero resultant force.
That is always correct, because moving objects need a resultant force.
That is always correct, because friction cannot act on cars.
That is not correct, because moving objects have no forces.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not always correct, because a car can move at constant speed with zero resultant force.
questions.explanation: A zero resultant force can mean constant speed. A common mistake is to think force is needed to keep moving.
questions.question: A student says, "If a car is moving, there must be a resultant force forwards." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: A zero resultant force can mean constant speed. A common mistake is to think force is needed to keep moving.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which equation is used to calculate work done?
options: work done = force x distance
work done = force / distance
work done = mass x speed
work done = distance / time
questions.explanation: Work done is force multiplied by distance moved in the direction of the force. A common mistake is to divide by distance.
questions.question: Which equation is used to calculate work done?
questions.senNote: Work done is force multiplied by distance moved in the direction of the force. A common mistake is to divide by distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A force of 10 N moves a box 3 m. How much work is done?
options: 7 J
questions.explanation: Use work done = force x distance, so 10 x 3 = 30 J. A common mistake is to add the values.
questions.question: A force of 10 N moves a box 3 m. How much work is done?
questions.senNote: Use work done = force x distance, so 10 x 3 = 30 J. A common mistake is to add the values.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate distance from work done and force
questions.stem: A worker does 240 J of work by pushing with a force of 60 N. How far does the object move?
options: 4 m
questions.explanation: Rearrange to distance = work done / force. So 240 / 60 = 4 m.
questions.question: A worker does 240 J of work by pushing with a force of 60 N. How far does the object move?
questions.senNote: Rearrange to distance = work done / force. So 240 / 60 = 4 m.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Link work done and energy transfer
questions.stem: What happens when work is done on an object?
options: Energy is transferred to or from the object.
Energy is destroyed by the object.
The object must become weightless.
The object must stop moving.
questions.correctAnswer: Energy is transferred to or from the object.
questions.explanation: Work done is a way of transferring energy. A common mistake is to think work destroys energy.
questions.question: What happens when work is done on an object?
questions.senNote: Work done is a way of transferring energy. A common mistake is to think work destroys energy.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about work done
questions.stem: A student pushes a wall with a force, but the wall does not move. They say, "Work is done on the wall because there is a force." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because work done needs movement in the direction of the force.
That is correct, because force alone means work is done.
That is correct, because walls store all the work.
That is not correct, because work is measured in newtons.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct, because work done needs movement in the direction of the force.
questions.explanation: Work done needs both force and distance moved. A common mistake is to ignore the distance moved.
questions.question: A student pushes a wall with a force, but the wall does not move. They say, "Work is done on the wall because there is a force." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Work done needs both force and distance moved. A common mistake is to ignore the distance moved.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is elastic deformation?
options: An object returns to its original shape when the force is removed.
An object breaks when the force is removed.
An object stays stretched forever after any force.
An object changes mass when stretched.
questions.correctAnswer: An object returns to its original shape when the force is removed.
questions.explanation: Elastic deformation is reversible. A common mistake is to confuse it with permanent deformation.
questions.question: What is elastic deformation?
questions.senNote: Elastic deformation is reversible. A common mistake is to confuse it with permanent deformation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: For a spring obeying Hooke's law, what happens when the force is doubled?
options: The extension doubles.
The extension halves.
The extension becomes zero.
The spring constant doubles.
questions.explanation: For Hooke's law, force is proportional to extension. A common mistake is to think the spring constant changes.
questions.question: For a spring obeying Hooke's law, what happens when the force is doubled?
questions.senNote: For Hooke's law, force is proportional to extension. A common mistake is to think the spring constant changes.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A spring has a spring constant of 40 N/m and an extension of 0.2 m. What force is applied?
options: 8 N
questions.explanation: Use F = k x e, so 40 x 0.2 = 8 N. A common mistake is to divide by the extension.
questions.question: A spring has a spring constant of 40 N/m and an extension of 0.2 m. What force is applied?
questions.senNote: Use F = k x e, so 40 x 0.2 = 8 N. A common mistake is to divide by the extension.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Choose a method to investigate a spring
questions.stem: A student investigates the extension of a spring. What should they change each time?
options: The force on the spring
The ruler scale
The room temperature only
The material of the clamp stand
questions.explanation: The force is changed and the extension is measured. A common mistake is to change something that is not the independent variable.
questions.question: A student investigates the extension of a spring. What should they change each time?
questions.senNote: The force is changed and the extension is measured. A common mistake is to change something that is not the independent variable.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Correct a misconception about Hooke's law
questions.stem: A student says, "A spring always obeys Hooke's law, no matter how large the force is." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because Hooke's law only applies up to the limit of proportionality.
That is correct, because all springs stretch evenly forever.
That is correct, because extension never changes.
That is not correct, because springs cannot store energy.
questions.correctAnswer: That is not correct, because Hooke's law only applies up to the limit of proportionality.
questions.explanation: A spring stops being directly proportional after the limit of proportionality. A common mistake is to assume the straight-line pattern continues forever.
questions.question: A student says, "A spring always obeys Hooke's law, no matter how large the force is." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: A spring stops being directly proportional after the limit of proportionality. A common mistake is to assume the straight-line pattern continues forever.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which equation calculates the moment of a force?
options: moment = force x distance from pivot
moment = force / distance from pivot
moment = mass x acceleration
moment = distance / time
questions.correctAnswer: moment = force x distance from pivot
questions.explanation: Moment is force multiplied by perpendicular distance from the pivot. A common mistake is to divide by distance.
questions.question: Which equation calculates the moment of a force?
questions.senNote: Moment is force multiplied by perpendicular distance from the pivot. A common mistake is to divide by distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is the pivot of a seesaw?
options: The point it turns around
The heaviest person on it
The total length of the seesaw
The force of gravity only
questions.explanation: A pivot is the turning point. A common mistake is to confuse the pivot with the force.
questions.question: What is the pivot of a seesaw?
questions.senNote: A pivot is the turning point. A common mistake is to confuse the pivot with the force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A 12 N force acts 0.5 m from a pivot. What is the moment?
options: 6 Nm
24 Nm
questions.explanation: Use moment = force x distance, so 12 x 0.5 = 6 Nm. A common mistake is to divide by 0.5.
questions.question: A 12 N force acts 0.5 m from a pivot. What is the moment?
questions.senNote: Use moment = force x distance, so 12 x 0.5 = 6 Nm. A common mistake is to divide by 0.5.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Explain effect of distance on moment
questions.stem: Why is it easier to turn a nut with a longer spanner?
options: The force acts farther from the pivot, giving a larger moment.
The nut has less mass with a longer spanner.
The force becomes zero with a longer spanner.
The pivot disappears when the spanner is longer.
questions.correctAnswer: The force acts farther from the pivot, giving a larger moment.
questions.explanation: A larger distance from the pivot gives a larger moment for the same force. A common mistake is to ignore distance.
questions.question: Why is it easier to turn a nut with a longer spanner?
questions.senNote: A larger distance from the pivot gives a larger moment for the same force. A common mistake is to ignore distance.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Use moments to balance a beam
questions.stem: A 4 N weight is 0.3 m from a pivot. What force is needed 0.6 m on the other side to balance it?
options: 2 N
8 N
questions.explanation: The first moment is 4 x 0.3 = 1.2 Nm. The balancing force is 1.2 / 0.6 = 2 N.
questions.question: A 4 N weight is 0.3 m from a pivot. What force is needed 0.6 m on the other side to balance it?
questions.senNote: The first moment is 4 x 0.3 = 1.2 Nm. The balancing force is 1.2 / 0.6 = 2 N.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which equation is used to calculate pressure on a surface?
options: pressure = force / area
pressure = force x area
pressure = area / force
pressure = mass x speed
questions.explanation: Pressure is force divided by area. A common mistake is to multiply force by area.
questions.question: Which equation is used to calculate pressure on a surface?
questions.senNote: Pressure is force divided by area. A common mistake is to multiply force by area.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: What is the unit of pressure?
options: Pa
N
J
m/s
questions.explanation: Pressure is measured in pascals, written as Pa. A common mistake is to choose N, the unit of force.
questions.question: What is the unit of pressure?
questions.senNote: Pressure is measured in pascals, written as Pa. A common mistake is to choose N, the unit of force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate pressure from force and area
questions.stem: A box exerts a force of 200 N over an area of 4 m2. What pressure does it exert?
options: 50 Pa
800 Pa
204 Pa
questions.explanation: Use pressure = force / area, so 200 / 4 = 50 Pa. A common mistake is to multiply force by area.
questions.question: A box exerts a force of 200 N over an area of 4 m2. What pressure does it exert?
questions.senNote: Use pressure = force / area, so 200 / 4 = 50 Pa. A common mistake is to multiply force by area.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Why do snow shoes help a person walk on soft snow?
options: They increase area, so the pressure on the snow is smaller.
They decrease area, so the pressure is smaller.
They remove the person's weight.
They make gravity stop acting.

questions.explanation: For the same force, a larger area gives a smaller pressure. A common mistake is to think pressure increases with area.
questions.question: Why do snow shoes help a person walk on soft snow?
questions.senNote: For the same force, a larger area gives a smaller pressure. A common mistake is to think pressure increases with area.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: A student says, "A sharp knife cuts better because it pushes with less pressure." Which response is best?
options: That is not correct, because a smaller area gives a larger pressure.
That is correct, because sharp knives have no force.
That is correct, because pressure is lower when area is smaller.
That is not correct, because pressure depends only on mass.

questions.explanation: Pressure increases when the same force acts over a smaller area. A common mistake is to reverse the effect of area.
questions.question: A student says, "A sharp knife cuts better because it pushes with less pressure." Which response is best?
questions.senNote: Pressure increases when the same force acts over a smaller area. A common mistake is to reverse the effect of area.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.stem: Which equation is used to calculate speed?
options: speed = distance / time
speed = distance x time
speed = time / distance
speed = force / mass
questions.explanation: Speed is distance divided by time. A common mistake is to multiply distance by time.
questions.question: Which equation is used to calculate speed?
questions.senNote: Speed is distance divided by time. A common mistake is to multiply distance by time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Calculate speed from distance and time
questions.stem: A cyclist travels 60 m in 12 s. What is the cyclist's speed?
options: 5 m/s
72 m/s
questions.explanation: Use speed = distance / time, so 60 / 12 = 5 m/s. A common mistake is to multiply the numbers.

kg
